en.extract:
The white-fronted ground tyrant (Muscisaxicola albifrons) is a species of bird in the tyrant flycatcher family Tyrannidae.
It is found in Bolivia, Chile, and Peru.


Taxonomy and systematics
The white-fronted ground tyrant was originally described as Ptyonura albifrons. Genus Muscisaxicola had been erected in 1837 and the species was moved to it in 1921.
The white-fronted ground tyrant is monotypic.


Description
The white-fronted ground tyrant is the largest member of its genus at 21.5 to 24 cm (8.5 to 9.4 in) long. The sexes have the same plumage. Adults have a white forehead ("front") that extends rearward in a "vee" above the eyes and a warm grayish brown crown on an otherwise blackish face. Their nape and back are brownish gray. Their wings are dark brown with wide white edges on the secondaries that show as a panel on the wing. Their tail is dark brown with grayish white edges on the outer feathers. Their throat, breast, and belly are pale gray with faint darker markings and their undertail coverts white. They have a brown iris, a black bill, and black legs and feet. Juveniles have less white on the forehead than adults, with cinnamon edges on the wing coverts, buff edges on the primaries and secondaries, and drab gray edges on the outer tail feathers.


Distribution and habitat
The white-fronted ground tyrant is found in the Andes from the Department of Ancash in north-central Peru south through the extreme northern tip of Chile into western Bolivia's La Paz Department. It primarily inhabits wetlands with cushion plants called bofedales and also nearby drier areas. In elevation it occurs above 3,700 m (12,100 ft), reaching 5,200 m (17,100 ft) in Peru and 5,600 m (18,400 ft) in Chile. It has been found roosting in glacier crevasses and nesting in ice caves.


Behavior


Movement
The white-fronted ground tyrant is a year-round resident.


Feeding
The white-fronted ground tyrant's diet and foraging behavior have not been detailed. Its diet is assumed to be arthropods and other invertebrates. It appears to forage mostly on the ground with short runs between prey captures.


Breeding
The white-fronted ground tyrant's breeding season has not been defined. Two juveniles were collected from Bolivia in the mid-twentieth century. The only known nest was found in October in Peru; it contained two nestlings. That nest was in a cavity on the ice at the edge of the Quelccaya Glacier in southern Peru's Cordillera Vilcanota. The researchers were not able to describe the nest at the time of discovery because it was deep in the cavity. Later visits to the site found what they believe to be nest remains consisting of small dark rootlets and much mammal hair thought to be that of vicuña (Lama vicugna).


Vocalization
As of April 2025 xeno-canto had only one recording each of the white-fronted ground tyrant's song and call. The Cornell Lab of Ornithology's Macaulay Library had the same call recording and five others also of calls. The song has not been described; the call is described as "a soft series of notes tip-tip-tip...".


Status
The IUCN has assessed the white-fronted ground tyrant as being of Least Concern. Its population size is not known and is believed to be stable. No immediate threats have been identified. It is considered uncommon to fairly common in Peru. "Despite being relatively common in a remote region, this species may be harmed by human impacts. Bofedales, which appear to be an important habitat for this species, are threatened by mining, overgrazing, and climate change (via changes in hydrology)."


References

The '''white-fronted ground tyrant''' ('''''Muscisaxicola albifrons''''') is a species of [[bird]] in the tyrant flycatcher family [[Tyrannidae]].<ref name=IOC15.1>{{cite web |url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/new/bow/flycatchers/ |title=Tyrant flycatchers |website=IOC World Bird List |version =v 15.1 | editor1-last=Gill | editor1-first=Frank | editor1-link=Frank Gill (ornithologist) | editor2-last=Donsker | editor2-first=David | editor3-last=Rasmussen | editor3-first=Pamela | editor3-link=Pamela Rasmussen |date=March 2025 |access-date=March 3, 2025 }}</ref>
It is found in [[Bolivia]], [[Chile]], and [[Peru]].<ref name=SACCcountries>Remsen, J. V., Jr., J. I. Areta, E. Bonaccorso, S. Claramunt, G. Del-Rio, A. Jaramillo, D. F. Lane, M. B. Robbins, F. G. Stiles, and K. J. Zimmer. Version 30 March 2025. Species Lists of Birds for South American Countries and Territories. https://www.museum.lsu.edu/~Remsen/SACCCountryLists.htm retrieved 30 March 2025</ref>

==Taxonomy and systematics==

The white-fronted ground tyrant was [[Species description|originally described]] as ''Ptyonura albifrons''.<ref>{{cite journal | last=von Tschudi |first=Johann Jakob |title=Avium conspectus quae in Republica Peruana reperiuntur et pleraeque observatae vel collectae sunt in ininere |  journal=Archiv für Naturgeschichte | volume=10 | issue=1 | pages=276–277 | publisher=Nicolai | date= 1844 |language=Latin | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/13704179 |access-date=April 19, 2025 }}</ref> Genus ''[[Muscisaxicola]]'' had been erected in 1837 and the species was moved to it in 1921.<ref>{{cite journal | last1=d'Orbigny |first1=Alcide |last2=de Lafresnaye |first2=Frédéric |title=Synopsis Avium | date= 1837 | journal=Magasin de Zoologie | volume=7 |page=65 | language=Latin |url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/37087510 |access-date=April 19, 2025 }}</ref><ref>{{cite book |last=Chapman |first=Frank M. |date=1921 |title=Bulletin 117: The distribution of bird life in the Urubamba valley of Peru |publisher=Smithsonian Institution |url=https://repository.si.edu/bitstream/handle/10088/10044/USNMB_1171921_unitfo.pdf |volume=117 |pages=90–91 |access-date=April 19, 2025 }}</ref>

The white-fronted ground tyrant is [[monotypic]].<ref name=IOC15.1/>

==Description==

The white-fronted ground tyrant is the largest member of its genus at {{convert|21.5|to|24|cm|in|abbr=on}} long. The sexes have the same plumage. Adults have a white forehead ("front") that extends rearward in a "vee" above the eyes and a warm grayish brown crown on an otherwise blackish face. Their nape and back are brownish gray. Their wings are dark brown with wide white edges on the [[Secondaries (birds)|secondaries]] that show as a panel on the wing. Their tail is dark brown with grayish white edges on the outer feathers. Their throat, breast, and belly are pale gray with faint darker markings and their undertail coverts white. They have a brown iris, a black bill, and black legs and feet. Juveniles have less white on the forehead than adults, with cinnamon edges on the wing [[coverts]], buff edges on the [[Primaries (birds)|primaries]] and secondaries, and drab gray edges on the outer tail feathers.<ref name=WFGT-BOW>Hardy, S. (2020). White-fronted Ground-Tyrant (''Muscisaxicola albifrons''), version 1.0. In Birds of the World (S. M. Billerman, B. K. Keeney, P. G. Rodewald, and T. S. Schulenberg, Editors). Cornell Lab of Ornithology, Ithaca, NY, USA. https://doi.org/10.2173/bow.wfgtyr1.01 retrieved April 19, 2025</ref><ref name=Peña>{{cite book | last1 =de la Peña | first1 =Martín R. | last2=Rumboll |first2=Maurice|  title =Birds of Southern South America and Antarctica | publisher =Princeton University Press | series =Princeton Illustrated Checklists | date =2001 | location =New Jersey | pages =Plate 76, map 76.9 | isbn =0-691-09035-1 }}</ref>

==Distribution and habitat==

The white-fronted ground tyrant is found in the Andes from the [[Department of Ancash]] in north-central Peru south through the extreme northern tip of Chile into western Bolivia's [[La Paz Department (Bolivia)|La Paz Department]]. It primarily inhabits wetlands with [[cushion plant]]s called [[bofedales]] and also nearby drier areas. In elevation it occurs above {{convert|3700|m|ft|abbr=on}}, reaching {{convert|5200|m|ft|abbr=on}} in Peru and {{convert|5600|m|ft|abbr=on}} in Chile.<ref name=WFGT-BOW/><ref name=Schulenberg>{{cite book | last1 =Schulenberg | first1 =T.S. | last2 =Stotz | first2 =D.F. | last3 =Lane | first3 =D.F. | last4 =O'Neill | first4 =J.P. | last5 =Parker | first5 =T.A. III | title =Birds of Peru | publisher =Princeton University Press | edition =revised and updated |series=Princeton Field Guides | date =2010 | location =Princeton, NJ | page =456  |isbn = 978-0-691-13023-1 }}</ref> It has been found roosting in glacier crevasses and nesting in ice caves.<ref name=Hardy>{{Cite journal | last1=Hardy | first1=S.P. | last2=Hardy | first2=D.R. | last3=Gil | first3=K.C. | date=2018 | title=Avian nesting and roosting on glaciers at high elevation, Cordillera Vilcanota, Peru | journal=Wilson Journal of Ornithology | volume=130 | issue=4 | pages=940–957 | doi=10.1676/1559-4491.130.4.940 | s2cid=91989752 | url=https://www.researchgate.net/publication/330485708 |access-date=April 19, 2025 | ref=none}}</ref>

==Behavior==
===Movement===

The white-fronted ground tyrant is a year-round resident.<ref name=IUCN/>

===Feeding===

The white-fronted ground tyrant's diet and foraging behavior have not been detailed. Its diet is assumed to be [[arthropod]]s and other invertebrates. It appears to forage mostly on the ground with short runs between prey captures.<ref name=WFGT-BOW/>

===Breeding===

The white-fronted ground tyrant's breeding season has not been defined. Two juveniles were collected from Bolivia in the mid-twentieth century.<ref name=WFGT-BOW/> The only known nest was found in October in Peru; it contained two nestlings. That nest was in a cavity on the ice at the edge of the [[Quelccaya Ice Cap|Quelccaya Glacier]] in southern Peru's [[Cordillera Vilcanota]]. The researchers were not able to describe the nest at the time of discovery because it was deep in the cavity. Later visits to the site found what they believe to be nest remains consisting of small dark rootlets and much mammal hair thought to be that of [[vicuña]] (''Lama vicugna'').<ref name=Hardy/>

{{birdsong|url=https://xeno-canto.org/species/Muscisaxicola-albifrons |species=the white-fronted ground tyrant}}
===Vocalization===
	
As of April 2025 [[xeno-canto]] had only one recording each of the white-fronted ground tyrant's song and call.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://xeno-canto.org/species/Muscisaxicola-albifrons |title=White-fronted Ground Tyrant - ''Muscisaxicola albifrons'' |author=<!--Not stated--> |date=April 19, 2025 |website=xeno-canto |access-date=April 19, 2025 }}</ref> The [[Cornell Lab of Ornithology]]'s [[Macaulay Library]] had the same call recording and five others also of calls.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://search.macaulaylibrary.org/catalog?taxonCode=wfgtyr1&mediaType=audio&sort=rating_rank_desc |title=White-fronted Ground-Tyrant ''Muscisaxicola albifrons'' |author=<!--Not stated--> |date=April 19, 2025 |website=Birds of the World |publisher=Cornell Lab of Ornithology |access-date=April 19, 2025 }}</ref> The song has not been described; the call is described as "a soft series of notes ''tip-tip-tip...''".<ref name=Schulenberg/>

==Status==

The [[IUCN]] has assessed the white-fronted ground tyrant as being of Least Concern. Its population size is not known and is believed to be stable. No immediate threats have been identified.<ref name=IUCN/> It is considered uncommon to fairly common in Peru.<ref name=Schulenberg/> "Despite being relatively common in a remote region, this species may be harmed by human impacts. Bofedales, which appear to be an important habitat for this species, are threatened by mining, overgrazing, and climate change (via changes in hydrology)."<ref name=WFGT-BOW/>
